design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate platforming levels, defeat bosses to collect treasure map pieces, upgrade fireball weapon via red orbs, reach final treasure
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (limited lives/continues) balanced against enemy variety and environmental hazards; fireball upgrade progression creates power fantasy escalation
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Play as Jinborov Karnovski, a muscular fire-breathing ex-circus strongman on redemption quest—former sinner given divine power to defeat evil wizard and retrieve ultimate treasure
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game heavily censored in Western release: entire narrative and cutscenes removed due to religious tones (divine redemption arc). Japanese version centers on moral redemption and spiritual quest; US version strips context to bare adventure. Karnov's physique and Soviet/Central Asian representation unusual for 1987 platformers. Game commits to difficulty philosophy differently by region (limited vs. infinite continues), showing intentional regional design variance.

difficulty.lives_system: NES version: Karnov takes two hits to die (turns blue after first hit, can recover with blue fireball orb). Japanese Famicom: takes one hit to die (reverts to arcade behavior). Both versions feature multiple lives pool.
difficulty.continues_system: NES (NA): unlimited continues with visible menu. Famicom (JP): two continues only, requires hidden input (Select+Start on Game Over screen). US manual claims three continues but source contradicts this—actual implementation is unlimited.
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based progression; no mid-level checkpoints. Boss defeats at level end grant treasure map pieces and progress.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Level select cheat code available (hold Right+Select+A+B on controller 1, press A on controller 2 to select stage 1-9, press Start). Regional differences create intentional difficulty variance: Famicom's limited continues and instant-death mechanics create higher stakes; NES's unlimited continues reduce tension but maintain pattern-memorization challenge.

progression.upgrade_system: Red orb collection upgrades fireball output: basic single fireball → dual fireballs → triple fireballs at maximum. Temporary items include Spike Bomb (destroys all on-screen enemies) and Shield (reflects enemy fireballs). NES-exclusive mechanic: blue fireball orbs restore health.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Boss defeats at level end unlock next level and grant treasure map pieces. Nine levels total, final boss 'The Wizard' (Famicom) or giant three-headed dragon (NES) guards final treasure map piece.
replayability_hooks: Level select cheat for practice runs
Three different endings based on performance metrics (Japanese version only; US version replaced with generic 'CONGRATULATIONS' text)
Regional differences encourage comparison playthroughs (Famicom vs. NES)
Boss pattern memorization encourages speedrun optimization

world_building_techniques: Intro cutscene establishes moral framework: Karnov is sinner-turned-servant of God, granted divine fire magic for redemption
Wizard Alakatay established as antagonist who cursed peaceful Cleamina region, triggering divine intervention
Boss variety (sword-wielding monks, dinosaurs, djinn, gargoyles, centipede women, skeleton warriors) suggests cursed landscape populated by magical corruption
Treasure map pieces serve as narrative progression—collecting fragments builds map to ultimate treasure
Multiple endings (JP only) reflect moral judgment based on player performance during quest

boss_design.philosophy: Each level features 'one or more bosses' (source: Wikipedia). Bosses serve as mandatory gates—defeating them progresses narrative and provides map piece. Pattern-based encounters requiring memorization of attack sequences. Final boss is significantly more powerful ('The Wizard' guards last map piece). NES version substitutes final boss with three-headed dragon in mechanically similar arena.
notable_bosses: The Wizard (Famicom final boss) - Described as 'powerful boss' who defends ultimate treasure map piece
Three-Headed Dragon (NES final boss) - Replaces Wizard in NES port; similar arena design suggests parallel mechanical complexity

faction_indicators: God as divine authority granting redemption through quest completion
Wizard Alakatay as corrupting force spreading monsters and curses across region
Town of Cleamina—innocent populace victimized by Alakatay's curse, motivating player intervention
danger_signals: Monster variety (monks, dinosaurs, djinn, gargoyles, tree monsters, will-o-wisps, rock creatures, centipede women, skeleton warriors) signals systematic magical corruption
Treasure map pieces as MacGuffins indicate incremental progress against overwhelming otherworldly threat
Boss gates as hard checks reinforce escalating danger progression
environmental_storytelling: Level design reflects magical curse theme—populated by diverse monsters suggesting widespread supernatural corruption rather than unified enemy faction
Levels 4 and 8 completely redesigned for NES (vs. arcade), suggesting environmental adaptation to platform constraints or narrative emphasis
Treasure as ultimate goal symbolizes redemption completion—material reward validating spiritual transformation

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor) - Karnov (NES) page
